column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Yttrium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/rare-earth/scandium-group/yttrium-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Yttrium surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Yttrium absorption and minimal reflectivity
Beam spot diameter for precise Yttrium surface cleaning with adequate energy density
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage on Yttrium surfaces
Fluence threshold for effective Yttrium cleaning
Fluence threshold for effective Yttrium contaminant removal without substrate damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient Yttrium ablation with controlled thermal effects
Optimal scanning speed for efficient Yttrium surface coverage without excessive overlap
Recommended number of cleaning passes for complete Yttrium surface treatment
Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform Yttrium surface cleaning

Typical range for industrial pulsed Nd:YAG lasers cleaning reflective metals like Yttrium; start at 100W to avoid thermal damage given Yttrium's high reflectivity (~70% at 1064 nm) and melting point (1522°C). Max limited to prevent substrate ablation. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs; similar to aluminum cleaning in J. Laser Appl. 25, 042005 (2013).

Effective for contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light. Recommended 2000 mm/s balances efficiency and safety for Yttrium's thermal conductivity (17 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax manuals; Opt. Lasers Eng. 47, 1069 (2009) for metal surfaces.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of metals; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) on Yttrium due to its reactivity. Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but higher cost. Source: Laser physics in Appl. Phys. A 101, 147 (2010); avoids plasma shielding issues at 1064 nm.

Typical for pulsed cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for Yttrium to enable fast processing while limiting heat accumulation in low-conductivity scenarios. Higher rates speed up but risk thermal damage. Source: Academic lit. in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 44, 435301 (2011); trade-offs noted for rare earth metals.

Fluence range for safe ablation of contaminants (e.g., oxides) on metals; Yttrium damage threshold ~1.5-2.5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity. Recommended 1.0 J/cm² for conservative cleaning. Uncertainty in exact Yttrium threshold; based on similar metals. Source: Ablation studies in Appl. Surf. Sci. 257, 5281 (2011).

Beam diameter for uniform coverage; 200 μm recommended for Yttrium surface prep, balancing fine detail and large-area efficiency. Smaller for precision, larger for broad cleaning. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Matrix series; J. Clean. Prod. 34, 57 (2012) for industrial metal applications.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete Yttrium cleaning to ensure removal without overexposure. Based on industry practice for reactive metals. Source: Case studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. 205, 479 (2010); minimizes risk of oxidation.

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or excessive overlap; 50% optimal for Yttrium to prevent uneven heating given its thermal properties. Typical range from process optimization. Source: Studies in Opt. Laser Technol. 42, 129 (2010); industry standards for scanned laser cleaning.
